1: ʾāḏām yĕlûḏ-ʾiššāh — 'man born of woman' — the phrase is a formal identification of human creatureliness: to be born is to be limited, mortal, and embedded in the generational chain of finitude. This exact phrase recurs in Job 15:14 and Job 25:4 (where Bildad uses it to deny human purity before God) and enters the NT in Galatians 4:4 ('born of woman, born under the law'). qĕṣar-yāmîm ûśĕḇaʿ-rōgez — 'short of days and full of agitation' — rōgez is turmoil, agitation, trembling (cf. Isaiah 14:3 where rest from rōgez is promised to the oppressed); the two phrases together give the double diagnosis of human existence: brief and troubled.
2: kĕṣîṣ yāṣāʾ wayyimmāl — 'like a flower he comes up and is cut off' — nāmal (to be cut off/wither) is used here as in Psalms 90:6 for the brief flourishing of human life; the flower metaphor for human transience appears also in Isaiah 40:6-8 ('all flesh is like grass... the flower of the field') and 1 Peter 1:24. wĕlō yāʿăḇōr — 'and he does not endure/pass over' — ʿāḇar normally means to cross/pass over; here the sense is 'he does not persist' or 'he does not abide,' reinforcing the shadow-image of v2b.
3: ʾap̄-ʿal-zeh p̄āqaḥtā ʿêneyḵā — 'yet on such a one do you open your eyes?' — p̄āqaḥ ʿênayim is to open the eyes with scrutinizing attention; the question is almost incredulous: this frail, ephemeral creature is the object of divine forensic attention? wĕʾōṯî ṯāḇîʾ bĕmišpāṭ ʿimmāḵ — 'and bring me into court with you?' — mišpāṭ + bôʾ ʿim = judicial proceeding; the disproportion between the creature's fragility (v1-2) and the solemnity of the divine court is Job's complaint.
4: mî-yittēn ṭāhôr miṭṭāmēʾ lō ʾeḥāḏ — 'who can bring something pure from something impure? Not one' — this is one of the most theologically dense one-line statements in Job. ṭāhôr/ṭāmēʾ (pure/impure) are the cultic purity categories; the rhetorical question with the answer 'not one' (lō ʾeḥāḏ) asserts the universal impossibility of purity arising from impurity. Paul in Romans 3:10-12 strings together similar ʾeḥāḏ / 'not one' statements from the Psalms; the logic of universal human uncleanness that undergirds the need for an external righteousness begins to take shape here.
5: ʾim ḥărûṣîm yāmāyw — 'since his days are determined/decided' — ḥāraṣ means to cut/decide; the passive participle ḥărûṣîm = 'decreed/fixed' (cf. Daniel 9:24-26 where the same root governs divinely determined periods of history). mispār ḥŏḏāšāyw ʾittĕḵā — 'the number of his months is with you' — the months of human life are God's to count, not the human's to determine. ḥuqqāyw ʿāśîṯā wĕlō yaʿăḇōr — 'you have set his limit that he cannot cross' — ḥōq (a fixed statutory limit) functions here as Job's argument for why God should ease up: if the limits are already fixed, there is no point maintaining adversarial pressure.
6: šĕʿēh mēʿālāyw wĕyiḥdāl — 'look away from him so he can rest' — šāʿāh (to look away, avert gaze) used with mēʿālāyw (from upon him) — the request is simply for divine inattention, the relief of not being watched. ʿaḏ-yirṣeh kĕśākîr yômô — 'until he completes, like a hired worker, his day' — the hired worker (śākîr) simile: a day-laborer's term of service is finite and acknowledged; at its end he has rest and his wages. Job is asking for a similar finitude of divine attention.
7: yēš tiqwāh lāʿēṣ — 'there is hope for a tree' — the word tiqwāh (hope, from qāwāh = to wait/hope) is used here for the first time in the chapter in a positive sense, though its application is to a tree rather than a human. The tree-hope becomes a foil for human hopelessness in vv10-12. wĕḥôṭer miggizʿô lō yĕḥdāl — 'and its new shoot will not stop' — ḥōṭer is a young shoot or sucker growing from a cut stump; gizʿāh is the stump; the tree regenerates from below ground even after being severed. The image of hope-through-remnant anticipates the messianic stump imagery of Isaiah 11:1 ('a shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse').
8: ʾim-yazzîn bāʾāreṣ šorĕšô — 'though its root grows old in the earth' — zāqan (to be old, to age) applied to a root hidden in soil; even if the visible part of the tree is gone, the aging root persists underground. wĕbĕʿāpār yāmûṯ gizʿô — 'and its stump dies in the dust' — gizʿāh (stump); māwet (death); ʿāpār (dust/earth). The imagery of death-in-dust will be reversed in v9 by the water-scent miracle, setting up the contrast with humanity in v10.
9: mērêaḥ mayim yapprîaḥ — 'at the scent of water it blossoms' — the tree's response to water is spontaneous and irrepressible: pāraḥ (to blossom/sprout) triggered by mere rêaḥ (smell/scent) of water. The sensory detail (smell, not taste or touch) emphasizes the tree's deep responsiveness to life-conditions. wĕʿāśāh qaṣîr kĕnāṭaʿ — 'and makes branches like a new plant' — the dead stump recovering to produce fresh growth ('like a new plant') is the image of resurrection without using the word; the trajectory from vv7-9 models renewal-from-death precisely to set up the contrast with human mortality in vv10-12.
10: wĕgāḇer yāmûṯ wayyeḥĕlaš — 'but man dies and falls helpless' — wayyeḥĕlaš from ḥālaš = to be weak/prostrate; the contrast with the tree's vigorous resprouting is stark. wĕgāwaʿ ʾāḏām wĕʾayyô — 'and man breathes his last — and where is he?' — gāwaʿ is the verb of expiration (breathing out the last breath); ʾayyô is the haunting interrogative 'where is he?', which carries the same existential weight as Cain's 'am I my brother's keeper?' (Genesis 4:9) and the angels' 'man, where are you?' (Genesis 3:9). The 'where?' question will echo in the Resurrection proclamation.
11: ʾāzĕlû mayim miyyām — 'as waters vanish from the sea' — āzal (to go away, to be gone; used very rarely, possibly related to a root meaning 'to exhaust'). The simile of water draining from a lake (yām can be any body of water) introduces the first of three water-failure images (v11: lake; v12a: river) that parallel human death. wĕnāhār yeḥĕraḇ wĕyāḇēš — 'and a river wastes and dries up' — ḥāraḇ (to be dry/desolate, the root of ḥōreḇ = desert) and yāḇēš (to dry up) together describe total desiccation with no prospect of return.
12: wĕʾîš šāḵaḇ wĕlō-yāqûm — 'man lies down and does not rise' — the water-failure simile completed: as water drains irreversibly, so man lies down irreversibly. The key phrase is the temporal clause: ʿaḏ-biltî šāmayim lō yêqîṣû — 'until the heavens are no more, they will not wake.' biltî is a negative particle (without/unless); the clause literally 'until the failure/absence of heavens, they will not awake.' This does not close the door on resurrection — it opens it: when the heavens finally end (as Isaiah 34:4; Job 51:6; Revelation 21:1 affirm they will), the sleepers will wake. The clause is eschatologically charged.
13: mî-yittēn bišʾôl taps̈pĕnēnî — 'O that you would hide me in Sheol!' — the optative mî-yittēn ('who will give?' = O that!) expressing a wish; Job's longing is for Sheol not as final destination but as temporary shelter from divine wrath. The phrase taps̈pĕnēnî (from ṣāp̄an = to hide/store) uses the same verb as Psalms 31:19 ('how great is your goodness which you have stored up [ṣāp̄antā] for those who fear you') — hiding in Sheol paradoxically connects to the divine storage of the righteous. ṯāšît lî ḥōq wĕtizkĕrēnî — 'set a fixed time for me and remember me!' — ḥōq (appointed/fixed limit) and zāḵar (remember); Job asks for a divine appointment in Sheol from which God will eventually summon him back, using the covenant-recall language of divine remembrance (cf. Genesis 8:1, 'God remembered Noah').
14: ʾim-yāmûṯ gāḇer hăyiḥyeh — 'if a man dies, will he live again?' — the resurrection question, the hinge of the chapter and one of the most theologically significant single-verse questions in the OT. ḥāyāh (to live) after māwet is either the question of resuscitation (will he simply revive?) or resurrection (will he be raised to new life?). The NT uses this exact logic-of-impossibility-now-made-possible structure in John 11:25 ('I am the resurrection and the life'). kol-yĕmê ṣĕḇāʾî ʾăyaḥēl ʿaḏ-bôʾ ḥălîp̄āṯî — 'all the days of my hard service I will wait until my release comes' — ṣāḇāʾ is military service/hard labor (used also in Isaiah 40:2 for Jerusalem's completed 'hard service'); ḥălîp̄āh is exchange/relief (from ḥālap̄ = to pass through, to change; used of a military rotation). Job envisions waiting in a post-mortem state of service until a divinely decreed moment of exchange/relief arrives — the closest OT parallel to a resurrection hope.
15: tiqrāʾ wĕʾānōḵî ʾeʿĕneh — 'you will call and I will answer' — the restoration of the divine-human dialogue broken by death; in Job's vision of the post-mortem state, God calls and Job responds. This is the inverse of the divine silence Job experiences in the present. tiḵsōp̄ lĕmaʿăśēh yāḏeyḵā — 'you will long for the work of your hands' — kāsap̄ means to long for/desire with yearning (used in Genesis 31:30 of Laban longing for his household gods; Psalms 84:2 of the soul longing for God's courts). God longing for his own handiwork (Job) is a remarkable attribution: the Creator is portrayed as yearning for the creature he made. This verse is the theological counterweight to all of Job's complaint — the ultimate frame is divine yearning, not divine indifference.
16: kî-ʿattāh ṣĕʿādî tispôr — 'for now you count my every step' — sāpar (to count/number) applied to steps; the divine surveillance experienced not as care but as obsessive prosecution. The contrast with v15 is sharp: there, God yearns; here (in present reality), God prosecutes. lō-ṯišmôr ʿal-ḥaṭṭāʾṯî — 'you do not overlook my sin' — in the hoped-for future relationship (v15), the implication is that God will choose not to prosecute every infraction; in the present, none escapes. The verse captures the present experience of grace-less divine scrutiny that makes existence unbearable.
17: ḥātûm bĕṣerôr pišʿî — 'my transgression sealed in a bag' — ṣārôr is a knotted pouch or bundle; pešaʿ (willful transgression) sealed inside it as if God is storing the evidence. wattipĕl ʿal-ʿăwōnî — 'and you stitch up my iniquity' — ṭāpal (to plaster/fasten over); the image is of God sealing and stitching shut the bag of Job's sins so nothing escapes the eventual reckoning. The forensic archive metaphor: God as keeper of perfectly preserved prosecutorial records.
18: wĕʾûlām hār-nôp̄ēl yibbôl — 'and surely a falling mountain crumbles' — the erosion section (vv18-19) uses geological imagery for the human condition under divine attrition. nāpal (to fall) + nāḇal (to decay/crumble); a collapsing mountain erodes over time rather than in a single dramatic event, mirroring the slow dissolution of Job's hope. wĕṣûr yeʿĕtaq mimmĕqōmô — 'and the rock is displaced from its place' — ʿātaq (to move on/pass over, used in Genesis 12:8 of Abram moving on); rocks considered immovable are yet dislodged by geological processes — another impossibility-become-possible that grounds Job's eschatological hope.
19: ʾăḇānîm šāḥăqû mayim — 'waters wear away stones' — šāḥaq (to wear down/pulverize, from which šāḥaq = fine dust/sky) used of water's slow grinding effect on rock; the same inexorable attrition applies to human hope. tišṭōp̄ sĕpîḥeyāh ʿăp̄ar-ʾāreṣ — 'its floods wash away the dust of the earth' — šāṭap̄ (to overflow/wash away) of floodwaters sweeping the topsoil away. wĕtiqwaṯ ʾĕnôš ʾibbadtāh — 'and the hope of man you destroy' — tiqwāh (hope, the same root used for the tree's hope in v7) now applied to man and declared destroyed; the geological erosion is the metaphor for the erosion of hope. Job returns to tiqwāh to close the circle from v7.
20: tĕqāp̄ēhû lāneṣaḥ wayyēlek — 'you overpower him once and he is gone forever' — lāneṣaḥ (forever, perpetually); the divine coup de grâce is delivered once (not repeatedly) but is permanent. mĕšanneh p̄ānāyw waṯĕšallĕḥēhû — 'you alter his face and send him away' — šānāh (to change); the alteration of the face at death — the features becoming unrecognizable, strange. The sending-away (šālaḥ) is a dismissal; life is a temporary summons and death is the dismissal after the audience.
21: yikbĕḏû bānāyw wĕlō-yēḏāʿ — 'his sons rise to honor and he does not know it' — yāḏaʿ (to know) is the verb of epistemological isolation; the father in Sheol cannot perceive the progress or regress of his children. wĕyiṣʿărû wĕlō-yāḇîn lāmô — 'they are brought low and he does not understand' — the double ignorance (honor and dishonor) is equally unknown; post-mortem existence as total epistemic isolation from earthly life. The verse is one of the OT's clearest statements of the disembodied state's disconnection from history.
22: ʾaḵ-bĕśārô ʿālāyw yikʾāḇ — 'but his flesh feels pain within him' — kāʾab (to feel pain/be grieved); the final verse describes the dying/dead man's last experience: exclusively inward, bodily pain. wĕnap̄šô ʿālāyw tiʾēḇāl — 'and his soul mourns over himself' — ʾāḇal (to mourn, used for mourning the dead); the soul's grief is reflexive — mourning itself rather than others. The chapter that began with man's brief, troubled existence (v1) ends with pain and self-mourning: the most existentially isolated possible image of death, which makes Job's expressed hope in vv13-15 all the more remarkable.
